_meta.coordinate_source: Wikipedia, Survey of India, Google Maps verified
_meta.notes: All coordinates verified against Wikipedia infoboxes and cross-checked with Google Maps. Timings are standard and may vary on festival days.

temples.name: Somnath Temple
temples.significance: First and foremost of the 12 Jyotirlingas. One of the oldest temples in India, rebuilt 16 times.

temples.name: Mallikarjuna Temple (Srisailam)
temples.significance: Rare temple that is both a Jyotirlinga and a Shakti Peetha. Located in Nallamala Hills.
temples.nearest_airport: Rajiv Gandhi International Airport, Hyderabad (220 km)

temples.name: Mahakaleshwar Temple
temples.significance: Only south-facing Jyotirlinga. Famous for Bhasma Aarti performed with sacred ash at dawn.
temples.nearest_airport: Devi Ahilyabai Holkar Airport, Indore (55 km)

temples.name: Omkareshwar Temple
temples.significance: Island shaped like Om (ॐ) on river Narmada. Houses two Jyotirlingas — Omkareshwar and Mamleshwar.
temples.nearest_airport: Devi Ahilyabai Holkar Airport, Indore (77 km)

temples.name: Kedarnath Temple
temples.best_time_to_visit: May to June; September to October
temples.significance: Highest of the 12 Jyotirlingas at 3,583 m. Part of Char Dham and Panch Kedar. Requires 20 km trek from Gaurikund.
temples.nearest_airport: Jolly Grant Airport, Dehradun (238 km by road)

temples.name: Bhimashankar Temple
temples.significance: Located in Sahyadri hills inside a wildlife sanctuary. Origin of river Bhima. Known for Giant Indian Squirrel (state animal).
temples.nearest_airport: Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport, Mumbai (115 km)

temples.name: Kashi Vishwanath Temple
temples.deity: Lord Shiva (Vishwanath — Lord of the Universe)
temples.significance: Most sacred Jyotirlinga. The new Vishwanath Dham corridor opened in 2021. Shiva is believed to grant moksha to those who die in Varanasi.
temples.nearest_airport: Lal Bahadur Shastri International Airport, Varanasi (25 km)

temples.name: Trimbakeshwar Temple
temples.significance: Origin of river Godavari. Linga has three faces representing Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva. Kumbh Mela held here (Nashik).

temples.name: Vaidyanath Temple (Baidyanath Dham)
temples.best_time_to_visit: July to August (Shravana Mela), October to March
temples.significance: Considered the most sacred abode of Shiva. During Shravana month, millions walk from Sultanganj (~110 km) carrying holy Ganga water.

temples.name: Nageshwar Temple
temples.deity: Lord Shiva (Nageshwar — Lord of Serpents) & Goddess Nagalai
temples.significance: First Jyotirlinga mentioned in Shiva Purana. Features a massive 25-metre statue of Lord Shiva.

temples.name: Ramanathaswamy Temple (Rameswaram)
temples.significance: Houses the longest temple corridor in the world (1,200 metres). One of the Char Dhams. Devotees take a ritual bath in 22 sacred teerthams (wells) inside.

temples.name: Grishneshwar Temple
temples.significance: Last and smallest of the 12 Jyotirlingas. Located near the UNESCO World Heritage Ellora Caves.

temples.name: Venkateswara Temple (Tirupati Balaji)
temples.best_time_to_visit: September to February (avoid peak festival rush)
temples.significance: World's richest and most visited religious site. Over 100,000 pilgrims visit daily. Part of 108 Divya Desams.
temples.nearest_airport: Tirupati Airport (15 km from base, Alipiri)
temples.nearest_railway: Tirupati Main Railway Station (22 km from Tirumala)

temples.name: Meenakshi Amman Temple
temples.significance: One of the most iconic temples in India. Features 14 gopurams (towers), 985 carved pillars, and the golden lotus tank. Around 15,000 visitors daily.

temples.name: Vaishno Devi Temple
temples.deity: Goddess Vaishno Devi (Maha Kali, Maha Lakshmi, Maha Saraswati)
temples.best_time_to_visit: March to October; avoid July–August (heavy rain)
temples.significance: Second most visited religious site in the world after Tirupati. Over 10 million pilgrims annually. A 14 km trek from Katra.

temples.name: Kamakhya Temple
temples.deity: Goddess Kamakhya (Devi Sati's womb — supreme Shakti)
temples.significance: Most powerful of all 51 Shakti Peethas. Marks the spot where Devi Sati's womb (yoni) fell. The temple has no idol — a natural rock fissure is worshipped.
temples.nearest_airport: Lokpriya Gopinath Bordoloi International Airport, Guwahati (23 km)

temples.name: Jagannath Temple
temples.significance: One of the original four Char Dhams (along with Dwarka, Rameswaram, Badrinath). World-famous Rath Yatra chariot festival. Non-Hindus not allowed inside.
temples.nearest_airport: Biju Patnaik International Airport, Bhubaneswar (60 km)

temples.name: Badrinath Temple
temples.best_time_to_visit: May to June; September to October
temples.significance: Part of both Himalayan Char Dham and original Maha Char Dham. One of 108 Divya Desams. Temple opens for 6 months annually.

temples.name: Yamunotri Temple
temples.best_time_to_visit: May to June; September to October
temples.season_open: Akshaya Tritiya to Yama Dwitiya (Bhai Dooj)
temples.significance: Source of river Yamuna. Starting point of Himalayan Char Dham Yatra. Requires a 6 km trek from Janki Chatti.

temples.name: Gangotri Temple
temples.best_time_to_visit: May to June; September to October
temples.significance: Origin of river Ganga. Most accessible of the four Himalayan Dhams — road reaches the temple directly. Gaumukh glacier (actual Ganga source) is a 18 km trek further.

temples.name: Dwarkadhish Temple
temples.significance: One of the four original Char Dhams. The legendary capital of Lord Krishna. 72-pillar temple, 5-storey tall with a 78-metre tall shikhara.

temples.name: Padmanabhaswamy Temple
temples.significance: World's wealthiest temple by assets (treasure valuing over ₹1 lakh crore). Non-Hindus not permitted. One of 108 Divya Desams. Devotees must wear traditional attire (mundu/saree).

temples.name: Ranganathaswamy Temple (Srirangam)
temples.deity: Lord Ranganatha (Vishnu in Shesha Shayanam pose)
temples.significance: Largest functioning Hindu temple complex in the world (156 acres, 7 concentric precincts, 21 gopurams). Asia's largest gopuram (73 m). First of 108 Divya Desams.

temples.name: Brihadeeswara Temple (Thanjavur)
temples.significance: UNESCO World Heritage Site (with Gangaikonda Cholapuram & Airavatesvara). Built by Raja Raja Chola I in 1010 AD. The 66m vimana casts no shadow at noon.

temples.name: Amarnath Cave Temple
temples.season_open: Guru Purnima to Raksha Bandhan (approx 45 days)
temples.significance: One of the holiest Hindu shrines. A naturally occurring ice Shivalinga forms every year, waxing and waning with the lunar cycle. Located at 3,888m.
temples.trek_from: Pahalgam (48 km traditional route) or Baltal (14 km short route)
temples.nearest_airport: Sheikh ul Alam International Airport, Srinagar (100 km from Pahalgam)

temples.name: Shirdi Sai Baba Temple
temples.significance: One of the most visited religious sites in India with over 25,000 visitors daily. A spiritual saint revered by both Hindus and Muslims. Prasadalaya (free meals for 50,000 daily).

temples.name: Siddhivinayak Temple
temples.significance: Most visited Ganesha temple in India. Receives lakhs of devotees on Tuesdays. One of the wealthiest temples in Maharashtra. Trunk of Ganesha faces right (symbolising wishes granted with difficulty but permanently).
temples.nearest_airport: Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport, Mumbai (25 km)

temples.name: Lingaraja Temple
temples.significance: Largest temple in Bhubaneswar ('Temple City of India'). Built in 11th century Kalinga style. Non-Hindus not allowed inside but a viewing platform is available.
temples.nearest_airport: Biju Patnaik International Airport, Bhubaneswar (3 km)

temples.name: Konark Sun Temple
temples.significance: UNESCO World Heritage Site. Conceived as a giant chariot of the Sun with 24 intricately carved wheels and 7 horses. Built by King Narasimhadeva I (1250 AD).
temples.nearest_airport: Biju Patnaik International Airport, Bhubaneswar (65 km)

temples.name: Akshardham Temple (Delhi)
temples.architecture_style: Vastu Shastra-based, carved from Rajasthani pink sandstone & Italian marble
temples.significance: Guinness World Record — world's largest comprehensive Hindu temple complex. Opened 2005. Built with no steel — entirely traditional stone craftsmanship by 11,000 artisans.

temples.name: Ram Mandir (Ayodhya)
temples.architecture_style: Nagara (Chandrakanta Somapura design), carved from Rajasthani pink sandstone
temples.significance: Believed to be the birthplace of Lord Ram. Inaugurated January 22, 2024. One of the largest and most significant Hindu temples built in modern times.
temples.nearest_airport: Maharishi Valmiki International Airport, Ayodhya (10 km)

temples.name: Guruvayur Temple
temples.deity: Lord Guruvayurappan (Krishna as a child — Unnikrishnan)
temples.significance: Known as 'Dwaraka of the South'. One of the most important Vaishnavite temples in Kerala. The deity is Vishnu in four-armed form. Famous for temple elephant herd (Guruvayur Devaswom elephants).

temples.name: Sabarimala Temple
temples.darshan_timings: Open during Mandalam–Makaravilakku (Nov–Jan) and Vishu; limited other times
temples.significance: Over 50 million pilgrims during Mandalam season — one of the world's largest annual pilgrimages. Pilgrims observe 41-day Vratham (austerities). Accessible only after a 4 km forest trek.

temples.name: Khajuraho Temples (Kandariya Mahadeva)
temples.significance: UNESCO World Heritage Site. 85 original temples (25 survive), built by Chandela dynasty (950–1050 CE). Famous for erotic and devotional sculptural panels.

temples.name: Nataraj Temple (Chidambaram)
temples.deity: Lord Nataraj (Shiva as Cosmic Dancer) & Goddess Shivakami
temples.significance: One of the five Pancha Bhuta Stalas (Akasha/Space element). The 'Chidambara Rahasyam' (secret space) is the holiest spot — Shiva worshipped as infinite space.

temples.name: Ekambareswarar Temple (Kanchipuram)
temples.significance: One of the five Pancha Bhuta Stalas (Prithvi/Earth element). Features a 59-metre Raja Gopuram. Ancient mango tree (Sthala Vriksha) believed to be 3,500 years old.

temples.name: Madhyamaheshwar Temple
temples.best_time_to_visit: May to June; September to October
temples.significance: Second of the five Panch Kedar. Shiva is worshipped as a navel-shaped (nabhi/middle) lingam. Located at base of Chaukhamba Peak with stunning Himalayan views.
temples.nearest_airport: Jolly Grant Airport, Dehradun (280 km by road)

temples.name: Tungnath Temple
temples.significance: Third of the five Panch Kedar. Highest Shiva temple in the world. The nearby Chandrashila summit (3,679 m) offers a 360° Himalayan panorama.

temples.name: Rudranath Temple
temples.significance: Fourth of the five Panch Kedar. Most remote of all Panch Kedar. Shiva's face is worshipped here. Known for surrounding alpine meadows (bugyals) filled with wildflowers.

temples.name: Kalpeshwar Temple
temples.deity: Lord Shiva (Kalpeshwar — matted hair/Jata of Shiva)
temples.best_time_to_visit: Year round (only Panch Kedar open in winter)
temples.significance: Fifth and final of the Panch Kedar. Only one accessible year-round. Shiva's Jata (matted hair) is worshipped here inside a cave.

temples.name: Adi Badri Temple
temples.significance: Complex of 14 temples. The primary Adi Badri temple contains a 3-feet black stone idol of Lord Vishnu. Accessible year-round unlike Badrinath.

temples.name: Ashtavinayak — Moreshwar Temple (Morgaon)
temples.significance: First and last Ashtavinayak (circuit starts and ends here). The most important of the 8 Ganesha temples. Ganesha rides a peacock here.

temples.name: Kalighat Temple
temples.significance: One of the four major Shakti Peethas. Sati's right toes are believed to have fallen here. Over 2,500 years old. One of the 51 Shakti Peethas.
temples.nearest_airport: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose International Airport, Kolkata (20 km)

temples.name: Ambaji Temple
temples.significance: One of the four major Shakti Peethas. Sati's heart is believed to have fallen here. Bhavnath Mahadev (Shiva) is the Bhairava companion. Over 1.5 million visitors during Navratri.
temples.nearest_airport: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel International Airport, Ahmedabad (185 km)

temples.name: Mahalakshmi Temple (Kolhapur)
temples.significance: One of 51 Shakti Peethas (eye or breast of Sati). Sati's eyes are believed to have fallen here. Uniquely, sunlight falls directly on the idol's face twice a year.

temples.name: Murudeshwar Temple
temples.significance: Features the world's second-tallest Shiva statue (123 feet / 37.5 m). The 237-feet Raja Gopuram has a lift. Set dramatically against the Arabian Sea.

temples.name: Raghunath Temple (Jammu)
temples.significance: Largest temple complex in northern India. Built by Maharaja Gulab Singh in 1835. Contains images of deities from all over India. Houses one of the largest collections of Saligrams.

temples.name: Dakshineswar Kali Temple
temples.deity: Goddess Kali (Bhavatarini — She who liberates the universe)
temples.significance: Where Sri Ramakrishna Paramahamsa had his divine visions and spiritual realisations. Built by Rani Rashmoni in 1855. Directly linked to Sri Ramakrishna Math and Swami Vivekananda.
temples.nearest_airport: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose Airport, Kolkata (15 km)

temples.name: Ujjaini Mahakali Temple (Nagpur)
temples.significance: One of the most revered Mahakali temples in Central India. Located at the geographic centre of India (Zero Mile). Visited by millions during Navratri.
temples.nearest_airport: Dr. Babasaheb Ambedkar International Airport, Nagpur (8 km)

temples.name: Vitthala Temple (Pandharpur)
temples.best_time_to_visit: Year round; Ashadhi and Kartiki Ekadashi
temples.significance: The supreme pilgrimage of Maharashtra Varkari tradition. Millions walk barefoot in the Wari procession twice a year (Ashadha and Kartika). Vitthal stands with hands on hips (Katyavalambita pose).

temples.name: Jwalamukhi Temple
temples.best_time_to_visit: March to June; September to November
temples.significance: One of 51 Shakti Peethas — Sati's tongue fell here. No idol — nine eternal natural gas flames (of different colours) are worshipped as manifestations of Devi.

temples.name: Naina Devi Temple
temples.significance: One of 51 Shakti Peethas — Sati's eyes fell here. Reachable by ropeway from Anandpur Sahib side. Visited by over 1 million pilgrims annually.

temples.name: Chamundeshwari Temple (Mysuru)
temples.significance: One of 51 Shakti Peethas. Reachable by road and 1,000-step staircase. The Dasara festival here is a UNESCO Cultural Heritage event drawing millions.

temples.name: Vishalakshi Temple (Varanasi)
temples.significance: One of 51 Shakti Peethas. Located near the Dashashwamedh Ghat. Often combined with Kashi Vishwanath darshan.
temples.nearest_airport: Lal Bahadur Shastri International Airport (25 km)

temples.name: Birla Mandir (Jaipur)
temples.architecture_style: Modern Rajput-Mughal fusion in white marble
temples.significance: One of the most beautiful modern temples in India. Built by the Birla family in 1988. Made of pure white marble quarried from Makrana. Translucent at night.

temples.name: Pushkar Brahma Temple
temples.significance: One of the very few temples in the world dedicated to Lord Brahma. The holy Pushkar Lake (Sarovar) is one of the five sacred lakes in Hinduism.

temples.name: Eklingji Temple
temples.deity: Lord Shiva (Eklingji — one Linga God, family deity of Mewar royals)
temples.significance: Family deity of the Mewar royal family. Complex of 108 temples, all encircled by a fortified wall. The primary Shivalinga has four faces (Chatarmukhi) made of black marble.

temples.name: Vrindavan — ISKCON Prem Mandir
temples.significance: Inaugurated 2012. White marble temple with illuminated gardens featuring scenes from Krishna Lila. One of the most visited religious sites in Braj (Mathura-Vrindavan).

temples.name: Tripura Sundari Temple (Banswara)
temples.significance: Ancient temple of the tribal Wagad region. Major Shakti shrine of southern Rajasthan. Revered by both Hindus and Adivasis.

temples.name: Trident Temple — Ujjain (Kal Bhairav)
temples.deity: Kal Bhairav (Fierce form of Shiva — Kotwal of Ujjain)
temples.significance: Kal Bhairav is considered the guardian of Ujjain. Uniquely, wine (Sharaab) is offered as prasad to the deity — the only temple in India where alcohol is an official offering.
temples.nearest_airport: Devi Ahilyabai Holkar Airport, Indore (55 km)

temples.name: Kamakshi Amman Temple
temples.significance: One of the most important Shakti Peethas associated with Adi Shankaracharya, who established the Sri Chakra here. The main deity is seated in a majestic Padmasana posture.

temples.name: Dakshineswar Kali Temple
temples.significance: Founded in 1855 by Rani Rashmoni. Famously associated with the mystic Ramakrishna Paramahamsa who served as its chief priest.
temples.nearest_airport: Netaji Subhash Chandra Bose International Airport (12 km)

temples.name: Badrinath — Yogadhyan Badri
temples.best_time_to_visit: May to June; September to October
temples.significance: One of the Panch Badri temples. Legend says King Pandu (father of Pandavas) performed penance here to cleanse himself of a curse.

temples.name: Badrinath — Bhavishya Badri
temples.best_time_to_visit: May to June; September to October
temples.significance: Part of Panch Badri. Believed that in future, when the current path to Badrinath becomes inaccessible due to landslide (collapsing mountains of Nara and Narayana), Badrinath will be worshipped here.

temples.name: Badrinath — Vridha Badri
temples.significance: The first place where Lord Vishnu appeared as a child to Narada Muni. Located 7 km before Joshimath on the main highway.

temples.name: Badrinath — Dhyan Badri
temples.best_time_to_visit: May to June; September to October
temples.significance: One of the Panch Badri temples. Houses an image of Vishnu in meditative pose. Located in the beautiful Urgam Valley close to Kalpeshwar Temple.

temples.name: Ashtavinayak — Mayureshwar Temple
temples.significance: The starting and ending point of the holy Ashtavinayak pilgrimage. The deity is depicted riding a peacock, which slew the demon Sindhu.

temples.name: Ashtavinayak — Siddhivinayak Temple (Siddhatek)
temples.significance: Second temple of the Ashtavinayak circuit. The only deity of the eight whose trunk points to the right. Lord Vishnu is believed to have successfully defeated Madhu and Kaitabha after praying here.

temples.name: Ashtavinayak — Ballaleshwar Temple
temples.significance: The only Ashtavinayak temple named after a devotee (the young boy Ballal). Built in stone with columns arranged in a star pattern.
temples.nearest_airport: Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport, Mumbai (110 km)

temples.name: Ashtavinayak — Varadavinayak Temple
temples.significance: Famous for the oil lamp (Nandadeep) which has been burning continuously since 1892. Devotees can personally perform pujas here.
temples.nearest_airport: Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport, Mumbai (82 km)

temples.name: Ashtavinayak — Chintamani Temple
temples.significance: Ganesha is believed to have retrieved the wish-fulfilling jewel (Chintamani) for Sage Kapila from the greedy prince Guna here. Associated with Maratha statesman Madhavrao Peshwa.

temples.name: Ashtavinayak — Girijatmaj Temple (Lenyadri)
temples.architecture_style: Rock-cut Cave (Cave #7 of Lenyadri Buddhist Caves)
temples.significance: The only Ashtavinayak temple located inside a Buddhist rock-cut cave complex (Cave 7). Reached by climbing 307 steps. Goddess Parvati performed penance in these caves to conceive Ganesha.

temples.name: Ashtavinayak — Vighneshwar Temple (Ozar)
temples.significance: Ganesha defeated the obstacle demon Vighnasur here. The temple is surrounded by massive stone walls and has a golden dome.

temples.name: Ashtavinayak — Mahaganapati Temple (Ranjangaon)
temples.significance: Lord Shiva is believed to have worshipped Ganesha here before destroying the demon Tripurasura. The temple architecture is designed so that the sun rays fall directly on the idol during noon.

temples.name: Kamakhya Temple (Guwahati)
temples.significance: Center of Tantric Shaktism. Womb (Yoni) of Sati fell here. During the annual Ambubachi Mela, the river Brahmaputra adjacent is believed to run red during the Goddess's menstruation cycle.
temples.nearest_airport: Lokpriya Gopinath Bordoloi International Airport (20 km)

temples.name: Ananthapadmanabhaswamy Temple
temples.significance: The richest place of worship in the world. Deity is reclining on the multi-headed serpent Anantha in the 'Anantha Sayana' posture. Regulated strictly under royal trust of Travancore.

temples.name: Sarangapani Temple (Kumbakonam)
temples.significance: One of the three major chariot temples of Tamil Nadu. Deemed third in rank among the 108 Divya Desams. Features a 12-story high Raja Gopuram.

temples.name: Bhudevi Sametha Venkateswara Temple (Tirupati)
temples.significance: Considered the spiritual capital of Andhra. First of the 108 Divya Desams in worship scale. Most visited Hindu temple with over 30 million visitors annually.

temples.name: Arulmigu Parthasarathy Swamy Temple (Chennai)
temples.deity: Lord Parthasarathy (Krishna as Charioteer of Arjuna)
temples.significance: One of the oldest temples in Chennai, originally built by Pallavas in 8th century. Notable as the only temple displaying Krishna with a moustache and sans weapons (as he vowed not to take up arms during the Kurukshetra war).

temples.name: Thirukkurungudi Nambi Temple
temples.significance: One of the 108 Divya Desams. Believed to be the place where Lord Vishnu acted as a disciple to learn the Tamil scriptures from Nammalvar. Known for Kaiser Kalaikoodam wooden carvings.

temples.name: Nava Tirupati — Alwar Thirunagari Temple
temples.significance: Birthplace of Saint Nammalvar, the primary Alvar saint. It is one of the nine Nava Tirupati temples (associated with Planet Jupiter - Guru). Features a hollow tamarind tree inside, believed to have housed Nammalvar in deep meditation for 16 years.

temples.name: Adikesava Perumal Temple (Thiruvattar)
temples.architecture_style: Kerala Dravidian Wood and Stone Style
temples.significance: An ancient temple which predates Padmanabhaswamy Temple, built on a higher platform. The deity is 22 feet long and made of Katusarkara Yogam (a traditional herb-stone paste mixture).

temples.name: Sri Rangam Ranganathaswamy Temple
temples.significance: The largest functioning temple complex in the world by area (156 acres). The first and primary Divya Desam temple. Features 21 Gopurams including the tallest in Asia (Rajagopuram, 73m).

temples.name: Bhavani Sangameshwarar Temple
temples.significance: Located at the holy confluence (Triveni Sangam) of three rivers: Bhavani, Kaveri, and the invisible Amudha river. Devotees perform rituals here to appease ancestors.

temples.name: Brihadeeswarar Temple (Gangaikonda Cholapuram)
temples.significance: UNESCO World Heritage Site. Built by Chola Emperor Rajendra I to commemorate his victory over Ganga dynasty kingdoms. Houses a giant 4-meter tall single-stone Shiva Linga, the largest in South India.
special_events: Annabhishekam (rice decoration of the Linga in Oct/Nov)
pilgrimage_circuits: UNESCO Great Living Chola Temples Circuit

temples.name: Sri Ksheeraraamalingeswara Swamy Temple
temples.significance: One of the five Pancharama Kshetras of Andhra Pradesh. The temple features a unique white milk-coloured Shiva Linga, and its Gopuram is one of the tallest in Andhra, standing at 120 feet.

temples.name: Kailasa Temple (Ellora Caves)
temples.significance: The largest rock-cut monolithic structure in the world, carved out of a single basalt cliff face from top to bottom by the Rashtrakutas in the 8th century.

temples.name: Mukteshwar Temple (Bhubaneswar)
temples.significance: Regarded as the 'Gem of Odisha Architecture' (circa 950 AD) for its exquisite archway (Torana) and detailed ceiling carvings showing Ganesha and the Saptamatrikas.

temples.name: Sakshi Gopal Temple
temples.significance: Houses a life-size idol of Lord Krishna. Traditionally, pilgrimage to Puri Jagannath Temple is considered incomplete without visiting Sakshi Gopal to bear witness.
special_events: Anla Navami (Only day Radha's feet are displayed)

temples.name: Rajarani Temple (Bhubaneswar)
temples.significance: Built in the 11th century using local red-gold sandstone called 'Rajarani'. Known for its elegant carvings of Dikpalas (direction guardians) and erotic couples, it has no active worship or main idol today.

temples.name: Karni Mata Temple (Deshnoke)
temples.significance: Famous worldwide as the 'Rat Temple', housing over 25,000 sacred black rats (Kabbas). Seeing a rare white rat is considered extremely auspicious.
special_events: Karni Mata Fair (twice a year during Navratri)

temples.name: Salasar Balaji Temple
temples.significance: Highly revered temple of Lord Hanuman, uniquely depicted here with a full beard and mustache. It is believed the self-manifested idol was found in a farmer's field in 1754.

temples.name: Mehandipur Balaji Temple
temples.significance: Famous for spiritual healing and ritualistic exorcism of negative energies and evil spirits. Features three deities: Balaji, Pretraj Sarkar (King of Spirits), and Bhairav.

temples.name: Shrinathji Temple (Nathdwara)
temples.darshan_timings: Eight dynamic daily darshans (approx. 5:30 AM – 7:30 PM intermittently)
temples.significance: Principal shrine of the Pushtimarg sect, founded by Vallabhacharya. Depicts Lord Krishna lifting Govardhan Hill with his left hand. The idol was moved here from Vrindavan in 1672 to protect it from Aurangzeb.

temples.name: Udupi Sri Krishna Matha
temples.significance: Established in the 13th century by Saint Madhvacharya. Uniquely, the deity is worshipped not directly, but through a silver-plated 9-hole window called 'Kanakana Kindi' associated with the devotee Kanakadasa.

temples.name: Kukke Subramanya Temple
temples.deity: Lord Kartikeya (Subramanya as Lord of Serpents)
temples.significance: Famous destination for Sarpa Samskara and Ashlesha Bali (rituals to rid of snake curses). Lord Subramanya merged here with the serpent king Vasuki to protect him from Garuda.

temples.name: Dharmasthala Manjunatha Temple
temples.significance: Unique temple administered by a Jain family (Heggade), where the temple priests are Vaishnavite Madhva Brahmins, and the presiding deity is Shiva. Known for charity, providing free meals to over 10,000 visitors daily.

temples.name: Sringeri Sharada Peetham
temples.significance: The first of the four mathas established by Adi Shankaracharya in the 8th century. Features the Vidyashankara temple with 12 zodiac pillars designed so that the sun rays hit them in sequence according to the months.

temples.name: Tulja Bhavani Temple (Tuljapur)
temples.significance: Family deity (Kuldevi) of Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj. One of the three and a half Shakti Peethas of Maharashtra. The self-manifested granite idol is dynamic and can be moved.

temples.name: Saptashrungi Temple (Nashik)
temples.architecture_style: Rock-cut hill shrine (with modern funicular trolley)
temples.significance: Located on a high hill with seven peaks (Saptashrunga). The massive 8-foot-tall idol of the Goddess is carved out of natural rock and has 18 arms holding various weapons.

temples.name: Chintpurni Devi Temple
temples.significance: One of the 51 Shakti Peethas. Sati's feet fell here. The presiding deity is worshipped in the form of a pindi (stone emblem) representing Chhinnamasta (the self-decapitated goddess).

temples.name: Bajreshwari Devi Temple (Kangra)
temples.significance: One of the 51 Shakti Peethas. Sati's left breast fell here. Historically looted for its immense gold treasures by Mahmud of Ghazni in 1009, and rebuilt completely after the 1905 Kangra earthquake.

temples.name: Tara Tarini Temple
temples.architecture_style: Kalinga Hilltop architecture (999 steps or ropeway)
temples.significance: One of the four major ancient Adi Shakti Peethas. Represents the breasts (Sthana Peetha) of Sati. Located on the Kumari hill banks of Rishikulya river.
special_events: Chaitra Mela (huge gathering on Tuesdays of Chaitra month)
temples.nearest_airport: Biju Patnaik International Airport, Bhubaneswar (165 km)

temples.name: Biraja Temple (Jajpur)
temples.significance: Famous Shakti Peetha where Sati's navel (Nabhi) fell. Presiding deity is a two-armed image of Durga piercing the chest of Mahishasura. Navratri features a rare independent chariot festival (Rath Yatra) for the Goddess.
special_events: Shodasa Puja (16-day festival during Durga Puja)
temples.nearest_airport: Biju Patnaik International Airport, Bhubaneswar (105 km)
temples.nearest_railway: Jajpur Keonjhar Road Railway Station (30 km)

temples.name: Danteshwari Temple (Dantewada)
temples.significance: One of the 51 Shakti Peethas. Sati's tooth (Danta) fell here. Presiding deity of the Kakatiya kings and Bastar tribes, carved in black stone.

temples.name: Kanakadurga Temple (Vijayawada)
temples.significance: Second largest temple in Andhra Pradesh, located on Indrakeeladri hill on the banks of Krishna river. Arjuna is believed to have performed penance to Lord Shiva here to obtain the Pasupatastra.
special_events: Dasara (huge festival with river bath processions)

temples.name: Tarapith Temple
temples.significance: Major Tantric center, famous for the adjoining cremation grounds and association with the saint Bamakhepa. Sati's eyeball/third eye (Tara) fell here.
temples.nearest_airport: Kazi Nazrul Islam Airport, Durgapur (95 km)

temples.name: Hanuman Garhi (Ayodhya)
temples.significance: A cave temple built in a fortress-like structure, custom dictates that before visiting the Rama Mandir, one must first pay respects to Hanuman here as he guards the city.

temples.name: Kanak Bhawan (Ayodhya)
temples.significance: Believed to be a palace gifted to Sita by Kaikeyi immediately after her marriage. Rebuilt in 1891 by Queen Vrishbhanu Kunwari of Orchha, the idols are adorned with gold crowns.

temples.name: Mundeshwari Devi Temple
temples.significance: Officially dated to 108 AD, it is recognized as the oldest functional temple in India, where worship has continued uninterrupted for nearly 2000 years. Famous for bloodless goat sacrifice where the goat is temporarily put to sleep by chanting mantra.
temples.nearest_airport: Lal Bahadur Shastri International Airport, Varanasi (100 km)

temples.name: Patan Devi Temple
temples.significance: The temple from which the city of Patna derives its name. One of the 51 Shakti Peethas. Sati's right thigh fell here. Divided into Badi (greater) and Chhoti (lesser) Patan Devi shrines.
temples.nearest_airport: Jay Prakash Narayan Airport, Patna (10 km)

temples.name: Deo Sun Temple
temples.significance: A rare monolithic stone Sun Temple that faces west instead of the traditional east. Dates back to the Umga dynasty (~15th century) with earlier foundations.
special_events: Chhath Puja (celebrated on a massive scale twice a year)
temples.nearest_railway: Anugrah Narayan Road Railway Station (20 km)

temples.name: Bhoramdeo Temple
temples.significance: Known as the 'Khajuraho of Chhattisgarh' due to its detailed erotic sculptured facades. Built by King Ramachandra of the Nagavanshi dynasty in the 11th century amidst the Maikal Hills.

temples.name: Six Abodes of Murugan — Subramaniyaswamy Temple (Tiruchendur)
temples.architecture_style: Dravidian Style (located on Bay of Bengal shoreline)
temples.significance: Second abode of Lord Murugan. Uniquely located directly on the seashore. Marks the place where Murugan defeated the demon Soorapadman.

temples.name: Six Abodes of Murugan — Dhandayuthapani Swamy Temple (Palani)
temples.architecture_style: Dravidian Hilltop style (693 steps, winch or ropeway)
temples.significance: Third abode of Lord Murugan. The main idol, created by Sage Bogar, is made of 'Navapashanam' (an alloy of 9 poisons that becomes healing medicine when bathed in milk).

temples.name: Six Abodes of Murugan — Swaminatha Swamy Temple (Swamimalai)
temples.architecture_style: Dravidian Hilltop style (60 steps representing 60 Hindu years)
temples.significance: Fourth abode of Lord Murugan. Marks the location where Murugan explained the meaning of the Pranava Mantra ('Aum') to his father Shiva.

temples.name: Six Abodes of Murugan — Thiruthani Murugan Temple (Thiruttani)
temples.architecture_style: Dravidian Hilltop style (365 steps representing days of the year)
temples.significance: Fifth abode of Lord Murugan. Represents the place where the Lord found peace and calm after defeating the demon Soorapadman, and where he married Valli.

temples.name: Six Abodes of Murugan — Solaimalai Murugan Temple (Pazhamudircholai)
temples.significance: Sixth and final abode of Lord Murugan. Celebrated as the site of the legendary 'fruit-seller' play between the poet-saint Avvaiyar and child Murugan under a rose-apple tree.

temples.name: Six Abodes of Murugan — Subramaniya Swamy Temple (Thiruparankundram)
temples.significance: First abode of Lord Murugan. A rock-cut temple carved into a massive granite hill. Marks the location where Murugan married Deivayanai, the daughter of Indra, in victory.

temples.name: Attukal Bhagavathy Temple
temples.significance: Famously called the 'Sabarimala of Women'. Holds the Guinness World Record for the largest annual gathering of women (over 3.7 million) cooking sweet Pongala offering on brick hearths around the city.
special_events: Attukal Pongala festival (10 days in Feb/March)
